title: Regularising contractual employees - the legal hurdles explained
p1:
Many believe that years of continuous service automatically give a contractual employee the right to become permanent.

But the law often sees this differently.
p2:
A long tenure, good performance, and a genuine need for the role may create expectations, but they do not always create a legal right to regularisation.

Courts have repeatedly held that public employers cannot bypass proper recruitment rules simply because someone has worked for many years. A temporary arrangement cannot become permanent only through the passage of time.
p3:
The real hurdle is the difference between a vacant post filled through a lawful process and a contractual engagement made for a specific purpose.

Regularisation depends on factors like the employer’s rules, the nature of the appointment, availability of sanctioned posts, and whether recruitment procedures were followed.

The key takeaway: job security and legal entitlement are not always the same thing.

Knowing this distinction can help both employees and organisations avoid costly misunderstandings.
conclusion: Should long-serving contractual employees get priority for regularisation, or must every permanent position always follow a fresh recruitment process?
